hook: You already know your one great thing is buried somewhere under a hundred merely good ones. This is the single question sharp enough to cut it free, plus the reason one small right move can eventually topple a mountain.

p: A single domino can knock over another domino about fifty percent larger than itself. Line up a chain where each one is half again as big as the last, and the growth is not a straight line where you add a little each time. The growth is geometric. It multiplies.

The physicist Lorne Whitehead worked the chain out in 1983, and the numbers are hard to believe. Start with a two-inch domino and keep making each one fifty percent bigger, and the eighteenth stands as tall as the Leaning Tower of Pisa, the thirty-first towers over Mount Everest, and the fifty-seventh nearly reaches the moon. All from a two-inch block and one small push.

That is the whole promise of the book. Extraordinary results are not about doing a thousand things. They are about finding the small lead domino you can actually knock over today, so it sets up the next, which sets up the next. When you trace remarkable achievements backward, you almost always find a first move that made the second possible. Success is built one falling piece at a time, so when the mountain feels impossibly far, you do not knock over the mountain. You find the small domino in front of you and you tip it.
sections.pull: Success is sequential, not simultaneous. It is built one falling domino at a time.

p: The beating heart of the book is a single question Keller wants you to memorize and use for the rest of your life. What is the ONE Thing I can do such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary? Every word in it is load-bearing.

The phrase ONE Thing forces a single answer instead of a list. I can do makes it action you actually control, not a wish. Such that demands a cause and effect. And the ending, everything else will be easier or unnecessary, is the leverage test. You are not hunting for something merely important. You are hunting for the domino that makes the other dominoes fall on their own.

Most people wake up and ask what they have to get done, then build a giant list and treat every item as roughly equal. But a to-do list is only everything you think you should do. It is not a plan. Turn it into a success list by asking which single thing makes the rest easier or irrelevant, and doing that one thing first. The question scales from your whole life down to this very hour. For a marriage, a sales goal, or your health, you simply narrow the frame.

Before you can focus, Keller says you have to clear away six lies that sound like wisdom but quietly wreck your attention. The first is that everything matters equally. It never does. In any list, a few tasks matter enormously and most barely matter at all, so achievers refuse to treat their list as a democracy.

The second lie is that multitasking works. It does not. You cannot give real attention to two things at once, you can only switch between them, and every switch costs time and quality. Texting while driving is dangerous not because your hands are busy but because your attention left the road. Multitasking is not doing more. It is doing more poorly. It is a tax, not a superpower.

The sixth lie is that big is bad. Keller flips it. The size of your thinking shapes the size of your results and the size of the person you must become to reach them, and much of what we call being realistic is just fear in a respectable disguise. So think big to pick the mountain, then go small to find the lead domino. Two more lies, about discipline and balance, matter so much they each get their own treatment next.

The third lie is that you need a disciplined life. You do not. You need just enough discipline to build one powerful habit at a time, then let that habit run on autopilot. Success is doing the right thing, not doing everything right. And a habit takes about sixty-six days of consistency to lock in, not the twenty-one days of popular myth, so pour your discipline into one habit until it runs itself, and only then add the next.

The fourth lie is that willpower is always there to summon. It works more like the battery on your phone, full in the morning and drawn down by every decision, temptation, and distraction you resist through the day. In a famous experiment, people forced to resist a bowl of warm cookies and eat radishes instead gave up on a hard puzzle after about eight minutes. Those allowed to eat the cookies persisted for nineteen. Same puzzle, less than half the stamina, because self-control is finite and the radish eaters had already spent theirs.

The lesson is about timing. Do not burn your peak willpower hours on email and other people's priorities, then attempt your most important work at four in the afternoon on an empty tank. Do your ONE Thing early, when the battery is full. Timing is not a detail. Timing is strategy.
sections.pull: Willpower is not a character trait. It is a battery, and every choice you make drains it.

p: The fifth lie is the most sacred one, the balanced life. A perfectly balanced life is a myth, Keller says, and chasing it is a recipe for mediocrity, because the middle is where nothing extraordinary ever happens. Anything meaningful demands concentrated blocks of time, and pouring yourself deeply into one thing means something else gets less of you in that moment. That is not a failure of balance. That is the price of doing anything that matters.

So you do not balance. You counterbalance. At work, counterbalance long. Lean hard into your ONE Thing and allow yourself to be deliberately out of balance in favor of your priority for extended stretches, because mastery and big results demand it. In your personal life, counterbalance short. Never let your health or the people you love drift too far out of whack for too long. Catch it quickly and lean back before real damage is done.

The word to hold onto is counterbalance. You are never standing perfectly still in the center. You are always leaning, adjusting, correcting, going long on your work priority and staying nimble at home. Give up the fantasy of every plate spinning at the same steady height. Know which plate matters most right now, give it what it needs, and trust yourself to swing back to the others before they crash.

Finding your ONE Thing is useless if the world steals the time to do it. The fix is time blocking. Carve out a specific, protected block for your ONE Thing, ideally a serious chunk in the morning when willpower is full, and treat it as the most sacred appointment on your calendar. Put it in first, before anything else claims the space, and let the rest of the day arrange itself around it. Block time to plan each week too, and a little time to rest, because the battery has to recharge.

Then defend that block the way a mother bear guards her cubs, because the world will not respect it for you. Colleagues want to meet, the inbox fills, and someone always has an urgent request they would love to make your problem. The thief of your time is almost always you, giving it away in small yeses to other people's priorities.

Keller names four thieves to fight. The inability to say no, because every yes to something is a quiet no to your ONE Thing. The fear of chaos, since real focus always leaves some mess behind, and that mess is a sign you are doing it right, not wrong. Poor health habits, because focus and energy run on your body. And an unsupportive environment, the wrong people and a distracting space, which you should re-engineer rather than try to overpower with willpower.
sections.pull: Every no you say to a distraction is a yes to your ONE Thing.

p: The last tool connects your grandest dream to the next hour. Start with your someday goal, the mountain. Then ask, based on that, what is the ONE Thing I can do in the next five years to be on track? Based on the five-year goal, the ONE Thing this year? Then this month, this week, today, and finally right now. You build a chain of dominoes that runs from a distant vision all the way back to something you can do in the next sixty minutes.

That is the whole point. A goal not connected to a present action is just a wish. It floats out there and never touches your life. Goal Setting to the Now turns an intimidating vision into a clear, doable next step, and every time you take that step you tip today's domino, which is lined up for this week's, and on up the chain to someday.

Underneath every technique sits one question Keller refuses to dodge. Why does any of it matter? The answer is purpose, a big why worth pouring your one life into. Purpose feeds priority, and priority drives productivity. Like Scrooge shown his own grave, live with the end in mind. Picture yourself looking back, decide which ONE Thing pursued with everything you had would have made your life extraordinary, and go knock over its first domino now.

chart.title: How far a chain of dominoes reaches
chart.subtitle: Each domino is 50 percent taller than the one before it, starting from a 2-inch block
chart.caption: Illustrative. Growing just 50 percent per step, it takes only 57 dominoes to nearly reach the moon. A short sequence of the right next steps compounds into something astronomical, which is exactly why the lead domino matters.

takeaways: Write the Focusing Question where you will see it daily: what is the ONE Thing I can do such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary?
Tomorrow, before you touch email or your phone, ask that question about your work and do that one thing first, while your willpower battery is full.
Open your calendar now and block protected time for your ONE Thing, in the morning if you can, then defend it and practice saying no graciously.
Pick one habit, just one, that would move your life forward and commit to it for sixty-six days before adding another.
Run Goal Setting to the Now on paper: someday, five years, one year, this month, this week, today, right now, ending on one action for the next hour.
Counterbalance instead of chasing balance: go long on your work priority, and correct quickly in your personal life before anything crashes.
Name your big why, and let that single purpose choose your one priority and shape your days.
